text: The laparoscopic approach involves four trocars, revealing the giant diverticulum originating from the sigmoid colon, now positioned in the greater pelvis during exploratory laparoscopy.

The surgeon performs a lateral-to-medial approach on the sigmoid colon, initially detaching the descending colon. Visible signs of diverticulitis are present at the lateral diverticulum margin, which are carefully dissected using blunt dissection and monopolar scissors.

The surgeon identifies and preserves the gonadal vessels and left ureter. Inflammatory signs, including edema and erythema, are visible in the lateral wall of the diverticulum at the recto-sigmoid junction.
The surgeon opens the peritoneum to access the vascular structures, identifying a window that connects with the lateral dissection area and verifying the location of the left ureter.

The surgeon performs vascular ligation at the recto-sigmoid junction using a 60mm Echelon stapler with a white cartridge. The dissection is completed with a ligature, and the division of the recto-sigmoid junction is executed using a 60mm Echelon stapler with a blue cartridge.
The surgeon extracts the specimen through a five-centimeter incision using an Alexis wound retractor protector, ensuring the incision remains open and protected during the extraction process.

The surgeon performs a tension-free colloculonic anastomosis using a 31mm circular EEA end-to-end anastomosis device. An intraoperative air leak test confirms the integrity of the anastomosis. A blank drain is placed in the surgical site.
